option_b: The cash you must have on hand to pay weekly payroll, rent, utilities, suppliers, and all other obligations
option_c: The total of accounts receivable minus accounts payable
option_d: Profits retained in the business over the prior fiscal year

question_text: The ratio of net profits to sales (profit-to-sales ratio) is computed by:
option_a: Dividing gross income by net profits
option_b: Dividing net profits by gross income
option_c: Multiplying gross profit by net profit percentage
option_d: Dividing net profit by gross profit margin

question_text: In the Summary of Profits to Sales, a net profit shown in parentheses such as ($2,490) means:
option_a: The job had a break-even result
option_b: The job produced a net loss
option_c: Retainage has not yet been collected, reducing the apparent profit
option_d: Overhead was not allocated to this job

question_text: Cash budgets are best described as:
option_a: Precise forecasts of exactly how much cash will be available each month
option_b: Attempts to judge the probabilities of the near-term future for planning purposes
option_c: Part of the general ledger required for financial reporting
option_d: Included as a schedule in the cash receipts journal

question_text: Factors that could invalidate even a well-prepared cash budget for a contractor include:
option_a: Building moratoriums
option_b: Dramatic changes in market demand
option_c: Changes in mortgage rates, home prices, or demand for new construction

question_text: A builder may willingly accept low-profit or even loss-producing work in a slack season primarily to:
option_a: Maintain good relationships with material suppliers
option_b: Cover operating expenses in slow seasons when more attractive work isn't available, and retain a productive labor force
option_c: Reduce the tax liability for the current year

question_text: The primary purpose of keeping a check register is to:
option_a: Replace the general ledger for small contractors
option_b: Classify payments in a logical manner and serve as the source document for cost analysis
option_c: Satisfy the bank's monthly reconciliation requirements
option_d: Record only the net amounts paid after discounts

question_text: 'Crossfooting' in a check register refers to:
option_a: Transferring totals to the general ledger at month-end
option_b: Adding distribution column totals across the page to verify they equal the total column, as a math error check
option_c: Reconciling the check register balance to the bank statement
option_d: Posting individual checks to the accounts payable register

question_text: When reconciling your checkbook balance, you should expect to see adjustments for:
option_a: Outstanding deposits and checks only
option_b: Automatic payments and special charges only
option_c: Returned checks only
option_d: Outstanding deposits and checks, automatic payments, special charges, and returned checks

question_text: To keep track of your checkbook balance when voiding a check written the previous month, you should:
option_a: Make the notation at the time the check was written but do not subtract anything from the balance
option_b: Subtract the amount from your current balance and remove the outstanding check from your bank reconciliation list
option_c: Ignore it; since the check is void it has no effect
option_d: Call the bank to deposit the amount back and then count it as a new deposit

question_text: Avoid writing checks made out to 'cash' because:
option_a: The IRS disallows all cash payments as business deductions
option_b: Business deductions for checks payable to 'cash' are questionable and harder to prove than checks drawn to a specific payee
option_c: Such checks cannot be deposited into a bank account
option_d: Cash checks violate Florida contractor licensing regulations

question_text: To reimburse a petty cash fund with a check written to yourself as a draw, you should:
option_a: Write the check to 'Cash' and record it as miscellaneous
option_b: Make the check out in your name and describe it on the stub as a 'draw'
option_c: Create a separate bank account specifically for petty cash
option_d: Record it as a salary expense and withhold taxes accordingly

question_text: When a voided check must be recorded in the check register:
option_a: It should be recorded at face value as a debit to miscellaneous expense
option_b: It is listed in sequence with a zero total, so that all checks are listed and accounted for
option_c: It is omitted from the register since no money was disbursed
option_d: It is posted to a separate void register maintained by the bookkeeper

question_text: Voided checks should be kept because:
option_a: They are required by federal tax law to be retained for seven years
option_b: They are supporting documents just like invoices, canceled checks, and statements
option_c: The bank may reactivate a voided check if not returned
option_d: They are needed to adjust the accounts payable register

question_text: In an automated check-writing system, a single entry should be able to produce:
option_a: A printed check and a general ledger entry only
option_b: A printed check, a cash disbursements journal entry, a general ledger summary entry, a running cash balance adjustment, and a job cost record entry
option_c: Only the check and a monthly summary report
option_d: A check, an accounts payable entry, and a bank reconciliation

question_text: The 'accounts payable double register method' uses:
option_a: Two separate checkbooks for payroll and operating expenses
option_b: Both a check register and an accounts payable register, with the AP register recording liabilities when incurred and the check register recording payment
option_c: Two separate entries for every accounts payable transaction
option_d: A pegboard system that writes the check and register entry simultaneously
source_ref: BGA Ch. 9 — Accounts Payable Double Register

question_text: The essential payroll records every employer must maintain include:
option_a: Canceled checks from suppliers for jobs on which employees work
option_b: Detailed earnings records, general ledger entries, job breakdowns, and copies of payroll tax returns
option_c: Income records showing justification for having full-time crews
option_d: Bank reconciliations showing all outstanding checks at month-end

question_text: When employees work on several different jobs, time cards should show:
option_a: Foreman approval for all crew time spent on each job
option_b: A breakdown of gross and net pay
option_c: A breakdown of all hours spent on each job
option_d: A summary of payroll taxes by job number

question_text: An employee's individual earnings record contains:
option_a: A breakdown by job for the entire year
option_b: A breakdown of dates, gross pay, and all deductions for each pay period, along with Social Security number, marital status, dependents, and pay rates
option_c: A worksheet to determine when the maximum for payroll tax has been reached
option_d: A record the employee keeps to verify the employer's calculations

question_text: Payroll accounting entries are made to:
option_a: The purchases journal and income journal
option_b: The general ledger only
option_c: The payroll journal only
option_d: Both the employee earnings record and the general ledger

question_text: When budgeting for payroll expenses, a contractor must remember that total labor cost includes, in addition to gross pay:
option_a: Employer portions of all payroll taxes, union dues, and the timing of payroll tax deposits
option_b: The employee's share of federal income tax withholding only
option_c: Workers' compensation insurance only
option_d: The cost of time cards and payroll software

question_text: The employer's liability for payroll taxes consists of:
option_a: The payroll taxes withheld from the employee's check that must be paid to the government
option_b: The additional amount the employer pays above what is taken from the employee's check
option_c: The combined employer and employee taxes due to be paid to the government
option_d: The liability insurance required to protect against injured employees

question_text: Federal unemployment insurance (FUTA) liability is reported on:
option_a: Form 941 quarterly
option_b: Form W-2 annually
option_c: Form 940 annually

question_text: Form W-4, the Employee's Withholding Allowance Certificate, must be:
option_a: Filed quarterly with the IRS
option_b: Completed by each employee; a new one is required whenever marital status, number of exemptions, or address changes
option_c: Prepared by the employer and given to the employee at year-end
option_d: Used to report wages and taxes in summary to the Social Security Administration

question_text: Form W-2, Wage and Tax Statement, is:
option_a: A form each employee fills out when hired
option_b: A form filed quarterly to report how much tax is due
option_c: Sent to each employee at year-end and to the Social Security Administration, reporting taxable income and taxes paid for the preceding year
option_d: Filed by an employee when the employer fails to send a year-end earnings summary

question_text: Form 941, Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return, summarizes:
option_a: FICA taxes withheld only
option_b: Taxable wages and provides for payment of income taxes or FICA due; liabilities include FICA withheld, FICA employer share, and federal income taxes withheld
option_c: Annual FUTA taxes

question_text: Overhead expenses differ from direct costs primarily because:
option_a: Overhead expenses are paid monthly while direct costs are paid weekly
option_b: Overhead expenses tend to remain fairly constant despite changes in sales volume, while direct costs change as volume changes
option_c: Overhead expenses are always fixed; direct costs are always variable
option_d: Overhead expenses are recorded only at year-end
source_ref: BGA Ch. 12 — Overhead vs. Direct Costs

question_text: Overhead is defined as:
option_a: Any expense paid by check rather than billed on account
option_b: The cost of items you need to conduct business that you cannot charge against a particular job
option_c: All expenses other than direct labor
option_d: The total of fixed and variable costs divided by the number of projects

question_text: Variable overhead expenses are:
option_a: Expenses associated with a specific project
option_b: Expenses not associated with any single job, though they vary with changes in sales volume
option_c: Expenses that remain constant regardless of how busy the company is
option_d: Expenses classified at year-end based on judgment

question_text: Which of the following is an example of a FIXED overhead expense?
option_a: Collection expenses
option_b: Union welfare payments
option_c: Salaries and wages for officers, estimators, and clerical employees

question_text: 'Small tools' as an overhead expense category includes:
option_a: Only tools valued under $25
option_b: Any tool purchase unless the value is high enough to treat it as a depreciable asset
option_c: Only tools purchased for office use
option_d: Tools rented for specific job sites

question_text: Equipment rented or used exclusively on job sites should be classified as:
option_a: Fixed overhead
option_b: Variable overhead
option_c: A direct cost
source_ref: BGA Ch. 12 — Equipment as Direct Cost

question_text: The entry to accrue an expense at month-end (expense incurred but not yet paid) is:
option_a: Debit cash, credit expense
option_b: Debit accounts payable, credit cash
option_c: Debit expense, credit accounts payable

question_text: The entry to reverse an accrual when the previously-accrued expense is paid involves:
option_a: Debit liability (Accounts Payable), credit expense account
option_b: Debit expense, credit cash
option_c: Debit expense, credit liability account

question_text: A prepaid asset account is used to record:
option_a: Monthly expenses for an expense paid all at once at the beginning of the period
option_b: Monthly expenses paid in advance due to a billing error
option_c: Depreciation on a capital asset
option_d: The down payment made on a fixed asset purchase

question_text: To record the initial payment of a prepaid expense such as annual insurance, the journal entry is:
option_a: Debit expense, credit cash
option_b: Debit prepaid assets, credit cash
option_c: Debit cash, credit prepaid assets
option_d: Debit accounts payable, credit prepaid assets

question_text: Indirect costs, as distinguished from direct costs and overhead, include:
option_a: Office rent, utilities, and clerical salaries
option_b: Freight, administrative payroll, and small tools — anything not billed to a specific customer
option_c: Gross labor, materials, and subcontractor costs

question_text: Materials inventory 'controlling' procedures include:
option_a: Trusting foremen to report missing items
option_b: Purchase orders, receiving reports, and physical counts matched against job cost records
option_c: Ordering 20% excess as a buffer
option_d: Delegating all purchasing to a single subcontractor

question_text: A 'job cost variance' occurs when:
option_a: Two different foremen work on the same project
option_b: Actual costs for a cost code differ from the budgeted (estimated) costs for that code
option_c: The project schedule slips more than 10 days
option_d: Subcontractor costs exceed 50% of the contract value

question_text: The 'percentage of completion' used to calculate earned revenue is most commonly measured by:
option_a: Number of days worked divided by contract duration
option_b: Cost incurred to date divided by total estimated cost at completion
option_c: Number of inspections passed divided by total inspections required
option_d: Billings to date divided by total contract value
source_ref: BGA Ch. 2 / ABC Manual Ch. 4 — Percentage of Completion

question_text: A contractor's 'overbilling' position on a project is most likely to create:
option_a: A cash flow problem for the contractor
option_b: A positive cash flow advantage for the contractor in the short term
option_c: An increase in the contractor's accounts receivable
option_d: A reduction in the contractor's bonding capacity

question_text: The 'restatement' of financial statements from completed-contract to percentage-of-completion, or vice versa, is performed to:
option_a: Avoid paying income taxes
option_b: Analyze your true income status and compare your financial performance under different accounting methods
option_c: Satisfy Workers' Compensation audit requirements
option_d: Correct errors in the original financial statements
source_ref: BGA Ch. 26 — Restatements by Accounting Methods

question_text: In construction accounting, 'deferred income' under the completed-contract method means:
option_a: Income that will never be recognized
option_b: Income received or billed on a job that is not yet complete and not yet recognized as earned income
option_c: Income held in escrow by the owner
option_d: Income that exceeds the contract price

question_text: 'Accrued expenses' on a balance sheet represent:
option_a: Expenses that have been paid but not yet recorded
option_b: Expenses that have been incurred but not yet paid or billed
option_c: Customer advance payments held in escrow

question_text: The critical path in a CPM schedule is defined as:
option_a: The path with the most activities
option_b: The longest sequence of dependent activities that determines the minimum project duration
option_c: The path with the highest cost activities
option_d: The sequence of activities requiring the most resources

question_text: 'Float' (slack) in a CPM schedule means:
option_a: The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion date
option_b: The additional cost required to accelerate a critical activity
option_c: The number of resources that can be added to shorten the schedule
option_d: The difference between planned and actual start dates

question_text: A 'Notice to Proceed' (NTP) formally:
option_a: Notifies subcontractors of the project start date
option_b: Authorizes the contractor to begin work and establishes the contract start date for schedule and time calculation purposes
option_c: Requires the contractor to submit a schedule within a specific number of days
option_d: Notifies the local building department that construction will begin

question_text: A 'punch list' in construction is:
option_a: A list of approved substitutions
option_b: A list of incomplete or deficient work items to be corrected before final completion and final payment
option_c: A list of subcontractors approved to work on the project
option_d: A list of change orders submitted by the contractor
source_ref: AIA A201 / ABC Manual — Punch List

question_text: 'Substantial completion' under AIA A201 is:
option_a: The date 90% of the contract value has been paid
option_b: The stage when the work is sufficiently complete so the owner can occupy and use it for its intended purpose
option_c: The date the contractor submits the final pay application
option_d: The date all punch-list items have been resolved

question_text: Under AIA A201, the Owner's right to occupy or use a portion of the Work prior to Substantial Completion requires:
option_a: The contractor's unreserved consent in all cases
option_b: Contractor consent (not unreasonably withheld), and agreement on insurance, security, and related matters
option_c: Automatic extension of contract time equal to the occupancy period

question_text: Under AIA A201, a 'Claim' is:
option_a: Any complaint by a subcontractor about the GC
option_b: A demand by one party seeking adjustment of contract terms, additional compensation, extension of time, or other relief
option_c: Only a request for additional money
option_d: A formal legal filing in court

question_text: AIA A201 requires the contractor to provide written notice of a claim within how many days after first recognizing the condition?
option_a: 7 days
option_b: 14 days
option_c: 21 days

question_text: The 'baseline schedule' in construction project management is:
option_a: The contractor's internal cost estimate
option_b: The approved schedule at project start, serving as the control document against which variances are measured
option_c: The owner's desired completion date

question_text: Look-ahead schedules (3-week or 6-week windows) are used to:
option_a: Replace the overall CPM schedule
option_b: Provide detailed near-term work plans, identify constraints, and coordinate upcoming activities
option_c: Satisfy owner's monthly reporting requirements

question_text: Under AIA A201, the Architect's role in contract administration is:
option_a: The Owner's representative with full authority to authorize changes without Owner approval
option_b: The Owner's representative for administration, making initial decisions on claims, certifying payments, and interpreting contract documents
option_c: An independent arbitrator binding both parties
option_d: A design professional with no construction administration role

question_text: Under AIA A201, the Contractor is responsible for:
option_a: Design of the work under performance specifications only
option_b: Supervising and directing the work, ensuring safety, coordinating subcontractors, and providing all labor, materials, equipment, and tools
option_c: Providing professional design services for structural systems
option_d: Hiring and paying the Owner's project manager

question_text: 'Supplementary Conditions' in AIA contract structure serve to:
option_a: Summarize the project schedule
option_b: Modify or supplement the General Conditions to address specific project, jurisdiction, or owner requirements
option_c: Replace the General Conditions for public projects
option_d: List all materials and workmanship standards

question_text: The AIA 'Contract Documents' typically include:
option_a: Only the drawings and specifications
option_b: The Agreement, General Conditions, Supplementary Conditions, Drawings, Specifications, Addenda, and other enumerated documents
option_c: Only the Agreement and Drawings
option_d: The Owner's program and design development documents

question_text: Under AIA A201, 'Work' is defined as:
option_a: Only physical construction by the Contractor's own forces
option_b: Construction and services required by the Contract Documents, including all labor, materials, equipment, and services to be provided by the Contractor
option_c: The portion designed by the Architect
option_d: All activities after Notice to Proceed

question_text: The 'Schedule of Values' serves as the basis for:
option_a: Setting the contractor's profit margin
option_b: Allocating the contract sum among work portions for evaluating payment applications
option_c: Establishing subcontractor bid prices

question_text: A 'Change Order' under AIA A201 is:
option_a: A written instruction to proceed with changed work pending formal agreement
option_b: A written instrument signed by Owner, Contractor, and Architect modifying the Contract Sum, Contract Time, or scope
option_c: A unilateral change directed by the Owner
option_d: An RFI that results in a scope change

question_text: A 'Construction Change Directive' (CCD) under AIA A201 is used when:
option_a: Owner and Contractor cannot agree on the scope of a change but the Owner needs the work to proceed immediately
option_b: The Contractor discovers a design error
option_c: A subcontractor requests a drawing change
option_d: The Architect issues a clarification with no cost impact

question_text: Under AIA A201, if the Contractor discovers concealed conditions materially different from those in the Contract Documents, the Contractor must:
option_a: Proceed and submit a change order request after completion
option_b: Provide prompt written notice to the Owner and Architect before conditions are disturbed
option_c: Stop all work until fully resolved
option_d: Correct the condition at no additional cost

question_text: 'General conditions' costs in a construction estimate refer to:
option_a: The AIA General Conditions document
option_b: Project-specific indirect costs such as field supervision, temporary facilities, job-site utilities, safety, and similar costs not assignable to a specific work item
option_c: The contractor's home office overhead and profit

question_text: A 'unit price' contract differs from a lump sum contract in that:
option_a: Unit price contracts are only for federal projects
option_b: The contractor is paid for actual quantities installed at pre-agreed unit prices, rather than a fixed total
option_c: Unit price contracts don't allow change orders
option_d: Unit price contracts fix total project cost at bid time

question_text: Under Florida law, a contractor must obtain a building permit:
option_a: Only for new construction over $50,000
option_b: Before commencing construction, alteration, repair, or demolition on structures covered by the Florida Building Code
option_c: Only for structural modifications
option_d: Only when required by local ordinance
source_ref: ABC Manual / Florida Building Code — Building Permits

question_text: Florida's Construction Lien Law (Chapter 713, F.S.) is designed to:
option_a: Protect contractors from owners who do not pay only
option_b: Protect all parties — owners, contractors, subs, sub-subs, laborers, and materialmen — by establishing a statutory process for securing payment
option_c: Establish priority of bank construction loans
option_d: Require all contractors to be licensed before filing any lien

question_text: Under Florida's Construction Lien Law, a Notice to Owner (NTO) must be served within:
option_a: 20 days of first furnishing labor or materials
option_b: 45 days of first furnishing labor or materials
option_c: 90 days of last furnishing labor or materials

question_text: Florida's 'Contractors' Final Payment Affidavit' is required:
option_a: At contract execution
option_b: Before the owner is required to make final payment, as a sworn statement that all subcontractors and materialmen have been paid (or listing unpaid amounts)
option_c: Within 30 days of substantial completion
option_d: Only when a lien has been filed

question_text: Under Florida Statute 489, a contractor who uses an unlicensed subcontractor when a license is required:
option_a: Is not responsible if the subcontractor provided false documentation
option_b: May be subject to disciplinary action including fines and license suspension
option_c: Is only responsible if an injury occurs
option_d: Has no liability if the sub's work passed inspection

question_text: AIA document A201 defines 'Substantial Completion' using the concept of:
option_a: 90% of contract value billed
option_b: The work being sufficiently complete for its intended use by the owner
option_c: All punch list items being resolved

question_text: 'Submittals' under AIA A201 are:
option_a: The contractor's invoices for payment
option_b: Shop drawings, product data, samples, and other items required by the Contract Documents for Architect review
option_c: The contractor's schedule updates

question_text: The Contractor's review and approval of submittals before forwarding to the Architect means:
option_a: The Contractor has independently verified all design dimensions
option_b: The Contractor has reviewed submittals for conformance with Contract Documents and confirmed field dimensions, quantities, and construction criteria
option_c: The Contractor has accepted full design liability
option_d: The Contractor may proceed with fabrication before Architect review

question_text: 'Requests for Information' (RFIs) are used to:
option_a: Request additional payment for unforeseen work
option_b: Seek clarification from the Architect/Engineer on design documents that are unclear, conflicting, or ambiguous
option_c: Notify the owner of schedule delays

question_text: Under the Florida Building Code, the contractor of record is responsible for:
option_a: Only structural elements
option_b: All work performed under the building permit, including work by subcontractors and sub-subcontractors
option_c: Workmanship only; subs are independently responsible for code compliance
option_d: Only work performed by the contractor's own employees

question_text: 'Substantial completion' versus 'final completion' means:
option_a: They are legally identical
option_b: Substantial completion means the work is sufficiently complete for its intended use (starting warranty and lien periods); final completion occurs after all punch-list items are resolved
option_c: Final completion precedes substantial completion
option_d: Only final completion triggers retainage release

question_text: The AIA A201 warranty period for correction of defective work is:
option_a: 6 months from Substantial Completion
option_b: One year from Substantial Completion
option_c: Two years from Final Completion

question_text: Florida's statute of repose for construction defects (Chapter 95, F.S.) means:
option_a: A defect claim can be brought at any time if the contractor knew of it
option_b: No action for construction defects may be brought more than 10 years after the date of actual possession or the date of issuance of a certificate of occupancy
option_c: All claims must be brought within 4 years of substantial completion
option_d: The statute applies only to structural defects

question_text: Under Florida Statute 558, before filing a construction defect lawsuit, the claimant must:
option_a: Obtain a court order permitting property inspection
option_b: Serve written notice on the contractor and allow an opportunity to inspect the alleged defect and offer a remedy or settlement
option_c: Wait at least one year after discovery before filing suit
option_d: File a complaint with the CILB before civil litigation
source_ref: Florida Statute 558 — Notice and Opportunity to Cure

question_text: The primary difference between 'design-bid-build' and 'design-build' delivery is:
option_a: Design-bid-build is only for public projects
option_b: In design-bid-build, design and construction are separate contracts; in design-build, a single entity is responsible for both
option_c: Design-build projects don't require building permits

question_text: 'Construction Management at Risk' (CMAR) differs from traditional design-bid-build in that:
option_a: The CM at Risk is hired as an agent with no financial risk
option_b: The CM at Risk provides preconstruction services and commits to a GMP, taking on financial risk for cost overruns above the GMP
option_c: The CM at Risk performs no self-performed work
option_d: The Owner retains all construction risk

question_text: A 'Guaranteed Maximum Price' (GMP) contract protects the owner by:
option_a: Guaranteeing the owner a fixed profit
option_b: Setting a ceiling on construction cost; the contractor bears the risk of cost overruns above the GMP
option_c: Eliminating the need for an independent cost estimate

question_text: The Florida DBPR / CILB has jurisdiction over:
option_a: Only commercial building contractors
option_b: Certified and registered contractors in Florida, including licensing, disciplinary actions, and renewal
option_c: All construction workers including laborers

question_text: Under Florida Statute 489, a 'certified' contractor differs from a 'registered' contractor in that:
option_a: Certified contractors have higher insurance requirements
option_b: A certified contractor holds a state-issued license valid throughout Florida; a registered contractor holds a local license recognized by the state but valid only within the issuing jurisdiction
option_c: Registered contractors can only work on residential projects
option_d: Certified contractors are not required to pass a state examination

question_text: Under Florida's contractor licensing law, a contractor's license number must be disclosed on:
option_a: Job sites only for projects over $50,000
option_b: All contracts, permit applications, and advertising materials
option_c: Only documents filed with the DBPR

question_text: Under Florida Statute 489, the qualifying agent is responsible for:
option_a: Financial management of the business only
option_b: All work performed under the business entity's license; the qualifying agent must actively supervise and be responsible for the operations of the business
option_c: An unlimited number of businesses simultaneously
option_d: Only work performed directly by the qualifying agent

question_text: Florida requires contractors to carry general liability insurance in order to:
option_a: Protect the contractor's personal assets from all business debts
option_b: Protect against claims for bodily injury and property damage arising out of construction operations, as required for licensure and often by contract
option_c: Replace Workers' Compensation on smaller projects
option_d: Satisfy IRS requirements for business deductions
source_ref: Florida Statute 489 / ABC Manual — GL Insurance

question_text: In Florida, a written construction contract over $2,500 must include:
option_a: A notarized signature from both parties
option_b: The contractor's license number, description of work, total price or basis for payment, and required statutory disclosures
option_c: A mandatory arbitration clause and lien waiver
option_d: Names and license numbers of all subcontractors

question_text: 'Value engineering' (VE) in construction is best defined as:
option_a: Reducing quality standards to cut costs
option_b: A systematic method of improving project value by analyzing functions and identifying alternatives that provide required functions at lower cost or improved performance
option_c: Increasing scope to improve long-term value
option_d: Substituting all materials with the cheapest alternative

question_text: 'Scope creep' in project management refers to:
option_a: The contractor gradually reducing quality
option_b: Gradual, uncontrolled expansion of project scope beyond original contract requirements, often without formal change orders
option_c: The owner's right to add work without additional payment
option_d: Delays from the contractor taking on too many projects

question_text: Florida's 'Home Improvement' contracting laws require contractors to:
option_a: Provide written contracts only for work over $10,000
option_b: Provide a written contract for work over $2,500, obtain required permits, comply with deposit restrictions; Florida provides a 3-business-day right of rescission for contracts solicited at the consumer's home
option_c: Complete all home improvement work within 30 days

question_text: A 'Notice of Commencement' (NOC) under Florida Statute 713.13 is:
option_a: Required only for projects over $500,000
option_b: A document recorded in public records before construction begins, identifying the owner, contractor, and project; it sets the lien law starting point
option_c: A permit issued by the building department
option_d: A bond required before the first construction loan draw

question_text: Misapplication of construction funds in Florida (Florida Statute 713.345) can result in:
option_a: Standard business practice with no criminal consequence
option_b: A criminal offense with penalties up to a felony depending on the amount misapplied
option_c: Only a civil breach of contract
option_d: A violation only if the contractor files for bankruptcy
source_ref: Florida Statute 713.345 — Misapplication of Construction Funds

question_text: A contractor's OSHA obligations extend to:
option_a: Only the contractor's direct employees
option_b: All workers on the project site, including subcontractor employees, to the extent the GC has control over the site
option_c: Only workers in high-risk activities
option_d: Workers only when the owner requires OSHA compliance in the contract
source_ref: OSHA / ABC Manual — Multi-Employer Worksite Policy

question_text: AIA A401 (Subcontract Agreement) primarily governs:
option_a: Owner and General Contractor relationship
option_b: General Contractor and Subcontractor relationship
option_c: Architect and General Contractor relationship
